title: Progressive breathlessness and weight loss in a smoker with heart failure on a diuretic

chart.oneLiner: 67M, former 40 pack-year smoker with HFrEF (EF ~40%), atrial fibrillation on apixaban, type 2 diabetes and hypertension, on furosemide, presents with several weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea, dry cough, and ~12 lb unintentional weight loss — the picture is muddied by his known heart failure and his diuretic.

opening: My breathing's been getting worse over a few weeks, and I've lost weight without even trying — my heart doctor already has me on a water pill, but it isn't helping this time.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 67-year-old man, thin and slightly breathless on exertion but comfortable at rest. Calm, cooperative, good historian. Frustrated that his usual heart-failure water pill hasn't fixed his breathing this time.

HPI: Several weeks of slowly progressive exertional dyspnea and a persistent DRY cough. ~12 lb unintentional weight loss over ~2 months with reduced appetite and fatigue. He has known heart failure and has had fluid build-ups before, but says this feels different — slower, with the weight LOSS (his prior heart-failure flares came with weight GAIN and leg swelling). His cardiologist increased his furosemide two weeks ago with NO improvement. NO fever, NO chills, NO orthopnea or PND this time, NO leg swelling, NO chest pain, NO palpitations beyond his usual irregular heartbeat, NO hemoptysis (mention hemoptysis, weight specifics, occupation, and clubbing ONLY if asked/examined). NO recent long travel or immobilization, NO unilateral leg swelling/pain. He DID miss two doses of his blood thinner last week because the pharmacy was late refilling it.
PMH: heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF ~40%, prior small MI 4 years ago); atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc elevated); hypertension; type 2 diabetes; gout; mild chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine ~1.2).
MEDS (reveal on direct questioning): amlodipine, lisinopril, metoprolol, FUROSEMIDE (recently uptitrated), apixaban (missed two doses last week), metformin, allopurinol, and occasional ibuprofen for gout flares. ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: former smoker ~40 pack-years (quit 5 yrs ago); worked in construction/shipyards for decades with likely ASBESTOS exposure (reveal only if asked about work/occupational exposure); rare alcohol; lives with his wife, independent in all activities, drives, climbs his own stairs at home (slower lately).
FAMILY: father had lung cancer. ROS: as above — constitutional weight loss/fatigue/poor appetite; otherwise negative.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: cachectic-appearing, comfortable at rest, no accessory muscle use at rest.
- Vitals: BP 126/79, HR 96 and IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR, RR 22, 37.1°C, SpO2 92% RA.
- Lungs: over the LEFT base — stony DULLNESS to percussion, decreased/absent breath sounds, DECREASED tactile fremitus, and reduced vocal resonance/egophony at the upper margin; right lung clear. No wheeze, no crackles on the right.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur, no S3, no rub. JVP NOT elevated (~6 cm) — argues against a decompensated-CHF fluid-overload picture despite his cardiac history.
- Neck: a small, firm LEFT supraclavicular lymph node if specifically palpated. Extremities: NO peripheral edema, NO calf tenderness/asymmetry; mild digital CLUBBING if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the asbestos/shipyard exposure, the family history of lung cancer, the weight-loss specifics, the missed anticoagulant doses, or clubbing unless asked about occupation, family history, weight, medications/adherence, or examined.
- The left-base effusion findings, the supraclavicular node, the JVP, and the clubbing are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed.
- The contrast he offers (weight LOSS and no edema/orthopnea, unlike his prior CHF flares) is given only if asked how this episode compares to past heart-failure flares.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer. Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 10.4 (normocytic anemia, MCV 88 — anemia of chronic disease/malignancy), WBC 7.6 (normal differential, no left shift — argues against empyema/parapneumonic), Plt 348.
labs.BMP: Na 130 (hyponatremia), K 4.0, Cl 92, HCO3 26 → anion gap = 130 − (92 + 26) = 12 (normal). BUN 32, Creatinine 1.6 (above his baseline 1.2 — mild AKI, likely diuretic/cardiorenal). Glucose 142.
labs.Calcium: Total calcium 11.4 with albumin 3.0 → corrected Ca = 11.4 + 0.8 × (4.0 − 3.0) = 12.2 mg/dL (hypercalcemia of malignancy / PTHrP physiology); ionized calcium confirmed high.
labs.BNP: BNP 290 pg/mL — only mildly/intermediately elevated. NOT the markedly high level expected to explain a large symptomatic effusion from decompensated heart failure; partially confounded by his AFib and CKD, so it neither confirms nor excludes a cardiac cause on its own.
labs.Thoracentesis: Pleural fluid is an EXUDATE by Light's criteria (pleural protein 4.6 / serum 6.8 = 0.68 > 0.5; pleural LDH 340 with serum LDH 180 → ratio 1.9 > 0.6 and pleural LDH > 2/3 the upper-normal serum limit). Lymphocyte-predominant, glucose normal, pH 7.34 (not loculated/empyema). Cytology POSITIVE for malignant cells (adenocarcinoma). AFB smear negative.
labs.CTchest: Large left pleural effusion with nodular/circumferential pleural thickening and an underlying ~3.5 cm spiculated left upper-lobe mass plus ipsilateral mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy; no saddle/segmental filling defect on the PE-protocol images and no RV strain — pulmonary embolism not demonstrated.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no organized P waves) at ~96 bpm with diffusely LOW QRS voltage (limb leads <5 mm, precordial <10 mm); no acute ST-elevation, no PE-pattern S1Q3T3 or new RBBB.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation with diffusely low QRS voltage. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial fibrillation with low QRS voltage
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 67-year-old man with known atrial fibrillation, heart failure, and a new large pleural effusion presents with progressive breathlessness.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (PA)
cxr.findings: Large left-sided pleural effusion with meniscus sign and blunted costophrenic angle; the size of a UNILATERAL effusion (with weight loss) favors malignancy over symmetric CHF; underlying mass may be obscured by fluid; possible mediastinal shift.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC file showing a large unilateral pleural effusion (meniscus sign).
cxr.imageDescription: Large left-sided pleural effusion with meniscus sign and blunted costophrenic angle; possible mediastinal shift.
searchTerms: chest radiograph large left-sided pleural effusion
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 67-year-old man presents with worsening left-sided breathlessness, weight loss, and dullness at the left lung base.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). This man has a competing story — a new unilateral effusion with weight loss versus his known heart failure on a diuretic — so address both. Also name THREE mechanisms/etiologies that produce an EXUDATIVE pleural effusion.
steps.rubric: Malignant pleural effusion, likely lung cancer with effusion (leading,6; accepts malignant effusion / mesothelioma given asbestos). Decompensated heart-failure (transudative) effusion — the genuine competitor given his HFrEF + diuretic (CANT-MISS-competitor,4). Pulmonary embolism — must-not-miss given AFib, malignancy, and missed apixaban doses (CANT-MISS,4). Parapneumonic effusion/empyema (core,2). Tuberculous effusion (core,2). Identifies the need to characterize the effusion as transudate vs exudate by Light's criteria to break the malignancy-vs-CHF tie (core,3). Names THREE exudate mechanisms/etiologies — e.g. malignancy, infection/parapneumonic, TB, pulmonary embolism, connective-tissue disease, pancreatitis, chylothorax (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on a CHF effusion and just up-titrating diuretics without characterizing the fluid (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Malignant pleural effusion (former heavy smoker, asbestos exposure, weight loss, unilateral effusion, hypercalcemia and anemia). The genuine competitor is a decompensated heart-failure (transudative) effusion given his HFrEF and recently up-titrated furosemide — but the weight LOSS (not gain), absence of orthopnea/edema, a non-elevated JVP, a UNILATERAL effusion, and only an intermediate BNP all argue against pure CHF. Must-not-miss: Pulmonary embolism (atrial fibrillation, malignancy, and two missed apixaban doses). Also reasonable: parapneumonic effusion/empyema and tuberculous effusion. THREE mechanisms/etiologies of an EXUDATIVE effusion: (1) malignancy (pleural invasion/lymphatic obstruction increasing capillary permeability), (2) infection — parapneumonic/empyema or tuberculosis, and (3) pulmonary embolism with infarction; other valid answers include connective-tissue disease, pancreatitis, and chylothorax. Characterize the fluid with Light's criteria to break the tie.

criticalActions.item: Decompensated heart-failure (transudative) effusion — the competing diagnosis

coreActions.item: Identifies need to characterize the effusion (transudate vs exudate by Light's criteria)
Names three mechanisms/etiologies of an exudative effusion

penalties.item: Anchors on a CHF effusion and up-titrates diuretics without characterizing the fluid

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CXR (CANT-MISS,3). Diagnostic + therapeutic thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis, Light's criteria, and cytology (CANT-MISS,5). BNP to weigh the CHF competitor (2). BMP (notes the AKI/hyponatremia) and calcium (catches hypercalcemia) (2). CBC (1). CT chest, ideally PE-protocol given the missed anticoagulation (2). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2); empiric diuresis before characterizing the fluid (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; BMP (assess the AKI and hyponatremia); calcium (catch the hypercalcemia of malignancy); BNP (helps weigh the CHF competitor); diagnostic AND therapeutic thoracentesis with pleural fluid protein/LDH for Light's criteria, cell count/differential, glucose, pH, and cytology; and CT chest — PE-protocol is reasonable given AFib with two missed apixaban doses, and it also characterizes the pleura/parenchyma.

criticalActions.item: Diagnostic thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis + Light's criteria + cytology

coreActions.item: BNP to weigh the heart-failure competitor
BMP and calcium (notes AKI, hyponatremia, hypercalcemia)

penalties.item: Empiric diuresis before characterizing the pleural fluid

requiredTests: Diagnostic thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis + Light's criteria + cytology
contraindicatedTests: Empiric diuresis before characterizing the pleural fluid

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's OWN 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and tie them to his presentation.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no P waves) at ~96 bpm WITH diffusely low QRS voltage (limb <5 mm, precordial <10 mm); no acute STEMI and no S1Q3T3/new RBBB to suggest massive PE. CREDIT: identifies atrial fibrillation and ties it to thromboembolic risk + the missed apixaban (6); recognizes the diffusely low QRS voltage and gives a differential (large effusion, infiltrative, COPD, obesity) (5); notes no acute ischemia and no PE strain pattern (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling the rhythm sinus or calling the study normal / missing either the AF or the low voltage (-3).
keyFindings: atrial fibrillation at ~96 bpm (irregularly irregular, no P waves)
diffusely low QRS voltage with a differential (large effusion, infiltrative, COPD, obesity)
no acute ischemia and no S1Q3T3/right-heart strain to suggest massive PE
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation at ~96 bpm with diffusely low QRS voltage.
commonMistakes: calling the irregularly irregular rhythm sinus
calling the study normal / missing the global low voltage
over-reading it as an acute STEMI or PE strain pattern
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation — irregularly irregular with no organized P waves — at ~96 bpm, with diffusely LOW QRS voltage (limb leads <5 mm, precordial <10 mm). The low voltage fits a large pleural effusion (also seen with infiltrative disease, COPD, or obesity). There is NO acute ST-elevation and NO S1Q3T3/new RBBB to suggest a massive PE. The AF matters two ways: it raises thromboembolic risk (and he missed two apixaban doses, reinforcing the PE concern) and it confounds his BNP, so the BNP cannot by itself attribute the effusion to heart failure.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation at ~96 bpm with diffusely low QRS voltage.
coreActions: atrial fibrillation at ~96 bpm (irregularly irregular, no P waves), tied to thromboembolic risk and the missed apixaban
diffusely low QRS voltage with a differential (large effusion, infiltrative, COPD, obesity)
no acute ischemia and no S1Q3T3/right-heart strain to suggest massive PE

penalties.label: calls the rhythm sinus, calls the study normal, or over-reads it as STEMI/PE strain

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = large unilateral (left) pleural effusion, blunted costophrenic angle, meniscus sign; the UNILATERAL pattern (vs the bilateral/symmetric effusions of CHF) plus the weight loss favors malignancy. CREDIT: identifies a pleural effusion (5); localizes the side and notes size/meniscus/blunted angle and that it is unilateral (3); comments on possible mediastinal shift or an underlying mass/opacity obscured by fluid (2).
keyFindings: localizes the side and notes it is unilateral with size/meniscus/blunted angle
comments on possible mediastinal shift or an underlying mass/opacity obscured by fluid
criticalFindings: Large left-sided UNILATERAL pleural effusion with meniscus sign and blunted costophrenic angle; possible mediastinal shift and an underlying mass obscured by fluid.
commonMistakes: Calling a symmetric CHF picture when the effusion is unilateral
Failing to comment on possible underlying mass / mediastinal shift
steps.idealAnswer: Large left-sided UNILATERAL pleural effusion with a meniscus sign and a blunted costophrenic angle. A unilateral effusion (rather than the bilateral, symmetric effusions typical of CHF), together with the weight loss, points toward malignancy. Comment on possible mediastinal shift and that an underlying mass may be obscured by the fluid — to be clarified on CT.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: large left-sided unilateral pleural effusion with meniscus sign and blunted costophrenic angle; possible mediastinal shift and underlying mass.

coreActions.item: localizes the side and notes it is unilateral with size/meniscus/blunted angle
comments on possible mediastinal shift or an underlying mass/opacity obscured by fluid

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The pleural fluid is an EXUDATE by Light's criteria with positive cytology, the CT shows a left upper-lobe mass with pleural nodularity, and the labs show hypercalcemia, anemia, hyponatremia, and a mild AKI. What is your diagnosis and reasoning? Commit, name the discriminating test, address the heart-failure competitor, and account for the other lab abnormalities.
steps.rubric: Malignant pleural effusion from lung cancer (5). Applies Light's criteria — pleural/serum protein 0.68 and LDH ratio 1.9 — to classify the fluid as an EXUDATE, which (with positive cytology) is the discriminating finding that excludes a transudative CHF effusion despite his HFrEF and diuretic (3). Integrates the multi-system labs as a coherent paraneoplastic/comorbid picture — hypercalcemia (PTHrP/malignancy), anemia of chronic disease, hyponatremia (diuretic +/- SIADH), and a mild AKI (diuretic/cardiorenal) — rather than as separate red herrings (bonus,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Malignant pleural effusion from a primary lung cancer. The discriminating step is Light's criteria: pleural/serum protein 0.68 (>0.5) and an LDH ratio of 1.9 (>0.6) make this an EXUDATE, and positive cytology confirms malignant cells — together these exclude a transudative heart-failure effusion even though he has HFrEF and is on furosemide (the non-elevated JVP, absent edema/orthopnea, weight LOSS, unilateral effusion, and only intermediate BNP already argued against CHF). The other labs cohere as a malignancy/comorbid picture rather than separate problems: hypercalcemia (corrected Ca 12.2) reflects PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia of malignancy; the normocytic anemia is anemia of chronic disease/malignancy; the hyponatremia is multifactorial (furosemide plus possible paraneoplastic SIADH); and the creatinine rise to 1.6 above his 1.2 baseline is a diuretic/cardiorenal AKI. Residual ambiguity — the exact histology and stage — is resolved with the CT-confirmed mass, cytology/histology, and oncologic staging.

bonusActions.item: Integrates hypercalcemia, anemia, hyponatremia, and AKI as a coherent paraneoplastic/comorbid picture

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition and safe-discharge planning?
steps.rubric: Per MGH thoracentesis / pleural fluid: therapeutic (large-volume) thoracentesis for symptomatic relief, removing fluid in a controlled volume with monitoring (CANT-MISS,6); send pleural fluid for cytology + Light's criteria (3); definitive control of the recurrent effusion — indwelling pleural catheter or chemical pleurodesis (4); treat the underlying malignancy / oncology referral + interventional pulmonary consult (3); manage the comorbid abnormalities — resume/reconcile apixaban for his AFib (and address PE risk from the missed doses), correct hypercalcemia with IV fluids ± bisphosphonate, recheck the AKI, and rate-control AF (3); safe-discharge/disposition — med reconciliation (anticoagulation adherence), outpatient oncology/IP follow-up with the pleural catheter taught to the patient/caregiver, goals-of-care discussion, and clear return precautions (CANT-MISS,4). PENALTY: removing a very large volume at once without monitoring (re-expansion edema) (-2); treating as a transudate with diuresis/anticoagulation alone (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Malignant pleural effusion: therapeutic (large-volume) thoracentesis for symptomatic relief, draining in a controlled volume with monitoring for re-expansion pulmonary edema; send the fluid for cytology and Light's criteria (already exudative). For the recurrent symptomatic effusion, arrange definitive control with an indwelling pleural catheter or chemical pleurodesis, and obtain interventional pulmonary and oncology consults to treat the underlying lung cancer. Manage the concurrent problems: reconcile and RESUME apixaban for his atrial fibrillation (he missed two doses — address the resulting PE/thromboembolic risk and rate-control the AF), treat the hypercalcemia of malignancy with IV isotonic fluids and a bisphosphonate, and recheck and support the diuretic/cardiorenal AKI rather than over-diuresing. SAFE DISCHARGE / disposition: this is an admission for the initial drainage and workup; plan discharge only after symptom relief with medication reconciliation (especially anticoagulation adherence after the missed doses), patient/caregiver teaching for the indwelling pleural catheter, scheduled oncology and interventional-pulmonary follow-up, a goals-of-care/serious-illness conversation, and explicit return precautions for worsening dyspnea, fever, or bleeding.

criticalActions.item: Therapeutic large-volume thoracentesis for relief, drained in a controlled volume with monitoring
Safe discharge: med reconciliation (anticoagulation adherence), indwelling-catheter teaching, oncology/IP follow-up, goals-of-care, return precautions

coreActions.item: Indwelling pleural catheter or pleurodesis for the recurrent effusion
Treat underlying malignancy / oncology + interventional pulmonary referral
Manage comorbid abnormalities — resume/reconcile apixaban for AFib, correct hypercalcemia, recheck AKI, rate-control AF

penalties.item: Removing a very large volume at once without monitoring (re-expansion edema)
Treating as a transudate with diuresis/anticoagulation alone

requiredActions: Therapeutic large-volume thoracentesis for relief, drained in a controlled volume with monitoring
Safe discharge: med reconciliation (anticoagulation adherence), indwelling-catheter teaching, oncology/IP follow-up, goals-of-care, return precautions
optionalActions: Indwelling pleural catheter or pleurodesis for the recurrent effusion
Treat underlying malignancy / oncology + interventional pulmonary referral
Manage comorbid abnormalities — resume/reconcile apixaban for AFib, correct hypercalcemia, recheck AKI, rate-control AF
unsafeActions: Removing a very large volume at once without monitoring (re-expansion edema)
Treating as a transudate with diuresis/anticoagulation alone
steps.disposition: Admit for drainage/workup; discharge with indwelling pleural catheter, reconciled anticoagulation, and close oncology/IP follow-up once symptoms controlled

physicalExamMappings.finding: Lungs: over the LEFT base — stony dullness to percussion, decreased/absent breath sounds, and DECREASED tactile fremitus; right lung clear. No wheeze, no crackles on the right.
Lungs: over the LEFT base — stony dullness to percussion, decreased/absent breath sounds, and DECREASED tactile fremitus; reduced vocal resonance/egophony at the upper margin; right lung clear.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Normal tactile fremitus and vocal resonance.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur, no S3, no rub. JVP NOT elevated (~6 cm) — argues against a decompensated-CHF fluid-overload picture despite his cardiac history.
Neck: a small, firm LEFT supraclavicular lymph node is palpable (Virchow-type node raising concern for intrathoracic/abdominal malignancy).
Extremities: NO peripheral edema and NO calf tenderness/asymmetry (argues against decompensated CHF and against an obvious DVT). Mild digital clubbing if examined.
Mild digital CLUBBING on inspection of the nails (chronic hypoxia / intrathoracic malignancy).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 67M, former 40 pack-year smoker with asbestos exposure, HFrEF on furosemide, and atrial fibrillation on apixaban (two missed doses), presents with weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea, dry cough, and ~12 lb weight loss. The genuine fork is a malignant exudative effusion versus a transudative heart-failure effusion, with pulmonary embolism as the must-not-miss. It is resolved toward Malignant pleural effusion by Light's criteria (exudate) with positive cytology and a CT-confirmed lung mass, while integrating concurrent hypercalcemia, anemia, hyponatremia, and a mild AKI.
keyHistoryClues: Weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea and dry cough with ~12 lb unintentional weight loss
This episode differs from his prior CHF flares — weight loss not gain, no orthopnea or edema, no response to up-titrated furosemide
40 pack-year smoking history and asbestos/shipyard occupational exposure; father with lung cancer
Two missed apixaban doses raising thromboembolic/PE concern
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Lungs: over the LEFT base — stony dullness, decreased/absent breath sounds, and decreased tactile fremitus; right lung clear.
Reduced vocal resonance/egophony at the upper margin of the left effusion.
JVP not elevated (~6 cm) — argues against decompensated CHF despite his cardiac history.
Small firm left supraclavicular lymph node.
No peripheral edema, no calf asymmetry; mild digital clubbing.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the irregularly irregular pulse and the cardiac comorbidity.
Elicit the focused history, including smoking, occupational asbestos exposure, missed anticoagulation, and the contrast with prior CHF flares — only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted exam to reveal the unilateral effusion signs, the non-elevated JVP, the supraclavicular node, and the absence of edema.
Build a differential with a real fork — malignant effusion vs transudative CHF effusion — and pulmonary embolism as the must-not-miss; name three exudate mechanisms.
Order a focused workup; interpret the patient's OWN EKG (atrial fibrillation with low voltage) and CXR (large unilateral effusion); send thoracentesis for Light's criteria and cytology.

Commit to Malignant pleural effusion via Light's exudate + positive cytology, account for the multi-system labs, and manage with therapeutic drainage, definitive pleural control, comorbidity management, and safe-discharge planning.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a CHF effusion and up-titrating diuretics without characterizing the fluid
Misreading the EKG rhythm as sinus or calling the low voltage normal
Removing a very large volume at once without monitoring (re-expansion edema)
Failing to address the missed anticoagulation and PE risk
managementPearls: Therapeutic large-volume thoracentesis drained in a controlled volume with monitoring
Safe discharge: med reconciliation (anticoagulation adherence), indwelling-catheter teaching, oncology/IP follow-up, goals-of-care, return precautions
criticalWorkupItems: Diagnostic thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis + Light's criteria + cytology
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation at ~96 bpm with diffusely low QRS voltage.
Large left-sided UNILATERAL pleural effusion with meniscus sign and blunted costophrenic angle; possible mediastinal shift and an underlying mass obscured by fluid.
criticalManagementItems: Therapeutic large-volume thoracentesis for relief, drained in a controlled volume with monitoring
Safe discharge: med reconciliation (anticoagulation adherence), indwelling-catheter teaching, oncology/IP follow-up, goals-of-care, return precautions
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation at ~96 bpm with diffusely low QRS voltage.
Large left-sided unilateral pleural effusion with meniscus sign and blunted costophrenic angle; possible mediastinal shift and underlying mass.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation at ~96 bpm with diffusely low QRS voltage.
Large left-sided unilateral pleural effusion with meniscus sign and blunted costophrenic angle; possible mediastinal shift and underlying mass.
imageInterpretationPearls: Read the rhythm before the morphology — an irregularly irregular tracing with no P waves is atrial fibrillation, and it reframes both the BNP and the embolic risk.
Diffusely low QRS voltage in a breathless patient should prompt a search for a large pleural (or pericardial) effusion before calling the film normal.
A UNILATERAL effusion with weight loss is malignancy until proven otherwise — CHF effusions are usually bilateral and symmetric.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions (smoking, adherence)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
